title: Abolition, slavery, and the Compromise of 1850
description: Test your understanding of life under slavery, the abolition movement, and the Compromise of 1850.
topic_path: Abolition, slavery, and the Compromise of 1850

assessment_items.question_content:
What was one result of **Nat Turner’s** rebellion, in which Turner organized a group of fellow slaves and killed 60 white people?



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: White Southerners were very scared in the wake of these attacks; they wouldn’t have wanted more slaves to be freed.

choices.content: White Southerners created stricter laws to police slaves’ behavior.
Virginia, Maryland, and North Carolina enacted gradual manumission policies.
Abolitionist John Brown, who many believed had incited Turner’s rebellion, was hanged.

assessment_items.question_content:
The antislavery **American Colonization Society** planned to deal with the issue of slavery by



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The American Colonization society did not have an agreement with the British government and would not have had the authority to send slaves to Canada.

choices.content: sending slaves to a colony on the west coast of Africa.
sending slaves to a community in Canada in an agreement they made with the British government.
creating a territory in present-day New Mexico where former slaves could live freely.

hints: William Lloyd Garrison, not Douglass, was the editor of The Liberator.

choices.content: a northern black abolitionist who had escaped slavery.
the first black senator from Massachusetts.
the founder and editor of the abolitionist newspaper *The Liberator*.

assessment_items.question_content:
What is the difference between **antislavery** and **abolition**?



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Both antislavery advocates and abolitionists believed that slavery was morally reprehensible.

choices.content: Antislavery advocates did not want slavery to spread any further (for example, to new states admitted to the Union) while abolitionists wanted an immediate end to slavery everywhere.
Antislavery advocates believed that slavery was morally wrong, while abolitionists did not.
Antislavery advocates wanted to confine slavery to the border states, while abolitionists wanted an immediate end to slavery everywhere.

assessment_items.question_content:
William Lloyd Garrison created **_The Liberator_**, which was 



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Liberator was not a novel, nor was it a precursor to Uncle Tom’s Cabin.

choices.content: an abolitionist newspaper.
an influential abolitionist novel that preceded Uncle Tom’s Cabin by ten years
a weekly journal that sought to unite pro- and anti-slavery advocates.

assessment_items.question_content:
The **Free Soil Party** was a political party mounted by 




[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Free Soil Party did not attempt to open the practice of slavery to new territories.

choices.content: The antislavery movement, which worked to prevent the expansion of slavery into new territories.
The temperance movement, which worked to enact prohibition laws in new states entering the union.
the proslavery movement, which hoped that slaveholders would have the freedom to practice slavery in new territories.

assessment_items.question_content:
The **Compromise of 1850** sought to settle disputes between north and south after the US acquired new territories in the Mexican-American War. It admitted California into the Union as a free state but also included a strict Fugitive Slave Law. What was the reaction to this compromise?



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: White Northerners did find the Compromise objectionable because they were asked to turn in escaped slaves. Many Northerners held a firm antislavery stance.

choices.content: The angry reactions on both the northern and southern sides led to even more division in the US and continued to set the stage for the Civil War.
Southern slaveholders were more outraged about the Compromise, while people in the north did not find it objectionable.
The Compromise eased tensions between the North and South and led to a brief period of harmony before the Kansas-Nebraska Act.

assessment_items.question_content:
What was one consequence of the **Fugitive Slave Law**?



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Southern slaveholders were not required to register escaped slaves with the federal government. The Fugitive Slave Law enhanced slaveowners’ rights.

choices.content: It made northerners directly complicit in slavery, prompting many of them to take a firmer stance against slavery.
It required southern slaveholders to register escaped slaves with the federal government, weakening states’ rights.
It created incentives for northerners who harbored fugitive slaves, resulting in more people in the North interacting with escaped slaves.

assessment_items.question_content:
Massachusetts senator Daniel Webster opposed the “Great Nullifier” John Calhoun, calling instead for



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Webster did not want the North to secede from the Union.

choices.content: national unity.
Northern states to secede from the Union, which he believed to be irrevocably tainted by slavery.
Southern states to secede from the Union, which he believed unfairly restricted the rights of slaveholders.
